participants.reasoning: The extracted text describes 54 randomized participants with different age/gender details and recruitment from a pediatric rheumatology setting, whereas the official record reports 64 participants and provides different or missing study details.
participants.extracted: Fifty-four children and adolescents with juvenile idiopathic arthritis (JIA) were randomized to an exercise group or a control group. The mean age was 13.9 years (range 8.8–21.6), and 41 were girls and 13 were boys. Most participants had polyarticular or extended oligoarticular JIA and were treated with methotrexate, TNF-blockers and/or prednisone; three had enthesitis-related or psoriatic arthritis. Participants were recruited from a pediatric rheumatology setting and had ongoing disease requiring repeated corticosteroid injections of lower-extremity joints. By the end of the study, some participants dropped out from the control group and from follow-up testing, mainly because they preferred the exercise group, lacked time, or experienced pain during testing.

interventions.reasoning: The extracted intervention matches the official programme in being a 12-week home-based exercise regimen with rope jumping, core and arm strength exercises performed three times weekly and tracked in diaries.
interventions.extracted: Participants in the exercise group completed a 12-week home-based physical therapy exercise programme performed three times per week. The programme included rope skipping, muscle strength exercises, core exercises, and free-weight exercises for the arms. It was designed as a weight-bearing fitness programme to improve physical activity, muscle strength, and well-being, and was carried out with exercise diaries used to record repetitions and adherence.
interventions.official: 1. Physical exercise group (intervention group, N = 33): an exercise programme with pictures, instructions, and a 12‐week diary were given to the exercise group at the first test occasion, along with performance instructions. The programme consisted of 100 two‐footed jumps with a rope, muscle strength core exercises, and muscle strength exercises with a load (0.5 kg to 2 kg) for the arms and shoulders, and 10 x 3 repetitions three times a week for 12 weeks. outcomes.reasoning: The extracted outcomes match the official list in timing and broadly in content, with additional measurement detail provided but no substantive contradiction.
outcomes.extracted: The trial planned to measure muscle strength, grip strength, physical fitness, pain, disability, and well-being/quality of life. Muscle strength was assessed with a handheld dynamometer in multiple muscle groups and expressed as torque (Nm) and, for comparison with normative data, as a percentage of predicted value; grip strength was measured with Grippit and compared with age- and sex-based normative categories; physical fitness was assessed with an adapted 6-minute step test, with power normalized to body weight (W/kg), heart rate, and Borg exertion recorded. Disability/function was measured with the Child Health Assessment Questionnaire (CHAQ; score 0 to 3) and health-related quality of life/well-being with the Child Health Questionnaire (CHQ-C87; domain scores). Pain was recorded as presence of pain during the test occasions using a 10-cm visual analogue scale (VAS), and pain-related well-being was also captured in the CHAQ/CHQ. Outcomes were collected at baseline, after the 12-week intervention (3 months/end of training), and at 6-month follow-up.
outcomes.official:
All the outcomes were measured at the baseline, 3 months after treatment, and after 6 months
Pain intensity: Child Health Questionnaire
Disability: Childhood Health Assessment Questionnaire
Quality of life: Child Health Questionnaire
Range of motion: plastic goniometer
Balance: Balance Reach Test
Muscle strength: handheld dynamometer
Activity level: step‐test
